title: We've Been Named the Top 100 Private Clubs in the World by PlatinumClubNet™
excerpt: The American Club Singapore has received Platinum Clubs of the World recognition for the second consecutive year, placing it among the top 1% of private clubs globally.
body:
We're delighted to share that The American Club Singapore has been awarded the Platinum Clubs of the World – City Clubs by PlatinumClubNet™ for the second consecutive year.

The Platinum Club® status represents the pinnacle of recognition in the private club world – placing us among the top 1% of private clubs globally.

This accolade reflects the Club's commitment to service excellence and exceptional Member experiences – made possible by our community and dedicated team.

title: Join our WhatsApp Channels & Stay Connected with Us!

body:
Get the first dibs on Club promotions and exclusive offers!

The American Club Main WhatsApp Channel — your one-stop channel for everything happening at the Club, from dining specials to event announcements.

F&B WhatsApp Channel — be the first to know about new menus, chef's specials, and dining promotions across our restaurants.

Scan the relevant QR codes on the Club's website to follow each channel.

title: Expat Living Reader's Choice Award 2025: The American Club as one of the Top 5 Best Social Clubs

body:
We are excited to announce that The American Club has been voted as one of the Top 5 Best Social Clubs in the Expat Living Reader's Choice Award 2025.

Thank you to everyone who voted for us, and to our incredible Members and Team — this achievement is made possible all because of you!

Your continued support of our Club is what motivates us to continue delivering world-class service excellence to every Member and Guest who walks through our doors. We strive to remain a Home Away from Home for generations to come.

Stay updated with what's happening at the Club by joining our WhatsApp Channels or following our Instagram page.

title: Inspiring Encounters: TAC Swimmers Meet World Champions
excerpt: American Club swimmers competed at the World Aquatics Swimming World Cup in November 2024, where they encountered elite athletes — and received a memorable gift from Olympic champion Léon Marchand.
body:
Although the Paris Olympics have concluded, the energy in the swimming world remains electric. In November 2024, the sport's elite gathered at the World Aquatics Swimming World Cup, hosted at Singapore's renowned OCBC Aquatic Centre. The event featured a thrilling short-course format in a modified 25-meter pool, offering a unique twist to the traditional competition setting.

This prestigious event showcased the world's top swimmers, creating an unforgettable experience for local athletes and fans. The American Club's Kai Alldritt, from the Senior Squad, qualified for the 100m and 200m Freestyle events. Despite juggling school commitments and missing preferred events, Kai competed internationally for the first time. Another talented swimmer, Kenzie Kaufenberg, met qualifying times but was ineligible to compete due to age restrictions.

Despite being unable to compete, the World Cup offered an invaluable opportunity for young Kenzie and peers to observe Olympians like Katie Douglass, Regan Smith, Katie Grimes, Duncan Scott, and Léon Marchand up close. Léon Marchand dazzled audiences with commanding performances months after his four-gold-medal triumph at the Paris Olympics, training under legendary coach Bob Bowman.

In a heartwarming moment, Léon's brother Eliott spotted American Club swimmers cheering enthusiastically. Noticing Kenzie's sign, Eliott pointed it out to Léon, who acknowledged the young fans with a wave. As world-class athletes broke records, they took time to acknowledge fans with smiles and waves from the pool deck.

The most memorable highlight came when Léon, awarded a special medal for exceptional performances, surprised everyone by gifting it to Kenzie in the stands. Overwhelmed with emotion, Kenzie held the medal, symbolizing Léon's generosity and encouragement. Scott Fowler described the moment as "one of the greatest displays of sportsmanship I've ever witnessed." Léon's humility and grace exemplify why these athletes inspire us — not solely for achievements but for their ability to uplift others.

The Club's Masters Swim Team proudly competed in the 2025 World Aquatics Masters Championships at the OCBC Aquatics Centre. This group, trained under Head Swim Coach Greg Fasala, showcased talents in individual and relay events. Many competed for the first time on an international stage alongside world-class athletes, underscoring the Club's commitment to nurturing talent and promoting swimming excellence at all levels.

title: Best Coffee Shops to Explore in Singapore
excerpt: Singapore's coffee culture blends traditional kopi stalls with modern specialty cafés. A guide to must-visit coffee shops across iconic neighbourhoods and hidden gems.
body:
Singapore's coffee culture has grown into a vibrant scene, with both traditional kopi stalls and modern specialty cafés dotting the island. Coffee plays an essential role in the everyday life of Singaporeans, serving as both a social activity and a daily ritual.

Discovering Singapore's Coffee Culture

Singapore's café scene is defined by its unique fusion of local traditions and global coffee trends. On one hand, you have the rich heritage of local kopi stalls, where robust coffee served with condensed milk is a staple of everyday life. On the other hand, the influence of third-wave coffee culture has introduced artisanal brewing methods, ethical sourcing, and an emphasis on high-quality, single-origin beans.

Third-wave cafés focus on coffee as an artisanal product. Cafés like Nylon Coffee Roasters and Common Man Coffee Roasters lead this change, offering thoughtfully crafted brews and educating customers on the origin and flavor profiles of their coffee.

Exploring Kopi: Singapore's Traditional Coffee

Found in kopitiams and hawker centers, kopi is brewed from robusta beans roasted with sugar and butter, giving it a rich, deep flavor. It's typically served with condensed or evaporated milk for a sweet and creamy finish.

Common kopi orders include Kopi (with condensed milk), Kopi Siew Dai (less condensed milk), Kopi Gah Dai (extra condensed milk), Kopi O (black coffee with sugar), Kopi O Kosong (black coffee without sugar), Kopi C (with evaporated milk and sugar), and Kopi C Kosong (with evaporated milk, no sugar).

Best Places to Enjoy Kopi

Ya Kun Kaya Toast — robusta-bean kopi paired with their famous kaya toast. Toast Box — smooth, aromatic kopi with condensed milk. Killiney Kopitiam — full-bodied traditional kopi. Chin Mee Chin Confectionery (204 East Coast Rd) — old-school kopi with charcoal-grilled buns.

Iconic Neighbourhood Cafés

Tiong Bahru — Tiong Bahru Bakery for buttery croissants and strong lattes, Plain Vanilla Bakery for cupcakes and smooth brews, The Glasshouse (Chijmes) for minimalist coffee and pastries.

Central Business District — Common Man Coffee Roasters for flat whites and brunch, The Providore Downtown for premium blends in an elegant setting, Punch for cold brews, Sarnies (Telok Ayer) for laid-back flat whites.

Specialty Brews

Nylon Coffee Roasters (Everton Park) for single-origin espresso, Kith Café for laid-back espresso, Alchemist The Heeren for rich espresso, Halcyon & Crane (Paragon) for bold creamy coffee, % Arabica for single-origin precision.

Hidden Gems

Kurasu (Waterloo St) for Japanese-inspired pour-overs, Bearded Bella (Craig Rd) for Australian-style coffee and brunch, Apartment Coffee (Selegie Rd) for intimate single-origin, Sago House Coffee (Duxton Hill) for coffee–cocktail hybrids.

Sustainable Cafés

Chye Seng Huat Hardware (Tyrwhitt Rd), Bettr Barista (New Tech Park), and The Wired Monkey (Amoy St) lead the way in ethical sourcing and eco-friendly practices.

Classic Pairings

Kaya toast with kopi, croissants with cappuccino, avocado toast with flat white, pancakes or eggs Benedict with latte. Singapore's coffee culture is a vibrant blend of tradition and modern innovation — there's always something new to try.

title: Must Try Local Food in Singapore for Expats
excerpt: Singapore offers a rich culinary tapestry reflecting Chinese, Malay, Indian and Peranakan influences. A guide to must-try local dishes and authentic hawker experiences for expats.
body:
Singapore is a true food lover's paradise, offering a rich tapestry of flavors that reflect the country's multicultural identity. With influences from Chinese, Malay, Indian, and Peranakan cuisine, Singapore's food culture is as diverse as its people.

A Myriad of Flavours

Chinese influence shows up in dishes like Hainanese Chicken Rice and Char Kway Teow. Malay heritage shines in Nasi Lemak and Satay. Indian cuisine contributes Roti Prata, Fish Head Curry, and the iconic pulled Teh Tarik. Peranakan fusion brings Laksa and Ayam Buah Keluak — a marriage of Chinese and Malay traditions.

Must-Try Local Dishes

Hainanese Chicken Rice — poached chicken with garlic-ginger rice. Try Tian Tian Chicken Rice at Maxwell Food Centre, famously praised by Anthony Bourdain.

Roti Prata — flaky pan-fried flatbread served with curry. The egg prata with fish curry is a popular order.

Kaya Toast — toasted bread with coconut–egg jam and butter, served with soft-boiled eggs and kopi. Ya Kun Kaya Toast has been serving this breakfast classic since 1944.

Char Kway Teow — flat rice noodles stir-fried with cockles, Chinese sausage, eggs and sweet soy. Best enjoyed at hawker centres for the signature 'wok hei'.

Nasi Lemak — coconut rice with sambal, fried chicken or fish, anchovies and peanuts.

Hokkien Prawn Mee — yellow and rice noodles in a rich prawn stock with squid and lime.

Laksa — coconut-based noodle soup. Visit 328 Katong Laksa for the iconic Katong style.

Chili Crab — large crabs in a sweet-tangy chili-tomato-egg sauce, perfect for dipping with mantou. Try Jumbo Seafood or Long Beach Seafood.

Snacks & Light Bites

Tutu Kueh & Putu Piring — steamed rice cakes with peanut, coconut or gula melaka. Haig Road Putu Piring is Michelin-recognized.

Goreng Pisang — golden banana fritters from hawker centres like Maxwell.

Nyonya Kueh — colourful Peranakan sweets, including Kueh Lapis and Kueh Salat. Try HarriAnns Nonya Table or Bengawan Solo.

Popiah — fresh spring rolls of turnip, carrot, lettuce, egg, peanuts and prawn. Kway Guan Huat has been hand-rolling for 80+ years.

Curry Puff — flaky pastry with spiced potato, chicken and egg. Old Chang Kee has been a household name since 1956.

Desserts & Drinks

Ice Kacang — shaved ice with syrups, evaporated milk and toppings like red bean, attap chee and palm seeds.

Chendol — shaved ice with coconut milk, pandan jelly noodles and gula melaka.

Teh Tarik — frothy pulled tea, a staple at Indian Muslim kopitiams.

Sugar Cane Juice — pressed fresh from the stalk at hawker centres.

Milo Dinosaur — iced Milo topped with extra Milo powder, a local cult favourite.

Dining Like a Local

Maxwell Food Centre — historic Chinatown hawker, home to Tian Tian Chicken Rice and Maxwell Fuzhou Oyster Cake. Old Airport Road Food Centre — Nam Sing Hokkien Fried Mee and Xin Mei Xiang Lor Mee. Lau Pa Sat — Victorian-era hawker centre that transforms into Satay Street at night.

For refined sit-down meals, try JUMBO (Clarke Quay) for Chili Crab and Black Pepper Crab, Spago Dining Room (Marina Bay Sands) for Wolfgang Puck's takes on local dishes, and Toast Box for breakfast in a café setting.

Hawker Etiquette

Reserve a table by 'choping' with a tissue packet. Order at the stall, pay on the spot (cash is common), and return your tray when done. Sharing tables is normal — queue politely; long lines usually mean great food.

Useful Phrases

Auntie/Uncle (respectful address for stall owners), Da bao (take away), Kopi/Teh + variations (Siew Dai / Kosong / Tarik). The OneMap website is a handy tool for finding the nearest hawker centre.

title: Country Clubs in Singapore: Popular Choices Among American Expats
excerpt: Discover popular country clubs in Singapore that cater to American expats — offering community, recreation, and networking opportunities for families relocating to the city-state.
body:
When relocating to a new country, finding a sense of community becomes essential — especially for American expats moving to Singapore. Country clubs offer more than just recreation; they provide a place to forge social connections, build professional networks, and enjoy family-friendly environments that can make the transition smoother.

Tips for Choosing a Country Club

Location matters — proximity to home, workplace and children's schools makes weekend activities and casual meet-ups more convenient.

Amenities and family programs are important — swimming pools, tennis courts, kids' sports programs, dining for children and weekend events.

For expats building professional networks, social and networking opportunities are key — wine tastings, sports leagues and speaker series provide fertile ground.

Membership costs vary significantly. Consider long-term value of activities, amenities and professional connections when evaluating costs.

Popular Country Clubs

The American Club — a cornerstone for American expatriates, often called a 'home away from home'. Located at 10 Claymore Hill, it offers family programming, networking events, state-of-the-art fitness, outdoor pools, and American-style dining. Full membership is open to American citizens and immediate families; associate membership is open to others.

Raffles Town Club (1 Plymouth Avenue) — upscale environment near Tanglin Road with fine dining, spa, wellness and family-friendly spaces.

Singapore Island Country Club (180 Island Club Road) — prestigious among golf enthusiasts, with tennis, pools, and nature trails.

National Service Resort & Country Club (10 Changi Coast Walk) — casual, affordable option with golf, bowling, swimming and family chalets.

The British Club (73 Bukit Tinggi Road) — central/western Singapore option with tennis, squash, swimming, kids' play areas, cultural events and family dining; open to non-British nationals.

Hollandse Club (22 Camden Park) — laid-back, diverse international membership; sports programs for children plus fitness classes and pools.

Tanglin Club (5 Stevens Road) — one of Singapore's oldest, established 1865; selective membership for professionals and diplomats with fine dining and squash.

Mandala Club (31 Bukit Pasoh Road) — contemporary club for creative professionals and entrepreneurs; membership by invitation.

b_together (50 Turf Club Road) — modern, family-focused, eco-conscious club with creative workshops, co-working space, children's play and eco-friendly dining.

Sports-Focused Clubs

Singapore Polo Club (80 Mount Pleasant Road) — horse-riding lessons, polo matches and social gatherings.

Singapore Cricket Club (Connaught Drive) — one of Singapore's oldest sports clubs, offering junior cricket, rugby and soccer programs at the Padang.

Conclusion

When choosing a country club, consider location, amenities, social opportunities and membership costs. The American Club's vibrant, welcoming community caters specifically to American expats with American and Canadian events, family activities and professional networking opportunities.

title: Best International Schools in Singapore for American Families
excerpt: Singapore offers a diverse range of international schools tailored to expat families. A comprehensive guide to top institutions, selection criteria, and programs suited for American families.
body:
Singapore's international schools serve a broad spectrum of educational needs, offering various curricula and philosophies to meet expat families and locals seeking alternative education systems. These institutions are known for world-class facilities and commitment to academic and holistic student development.

Choosing the Right School

Curriculum and academic excellence — choosing between American, British, IB or other curricula depends on a child's educational and career aspirations.

Facilities and extracurricular activities — state-of-the-art classrooms, advanced science labs, sports complexes and arts facilities support well-rounded development.

Location and accessibility — proximity to home or workplace significantly impacts daily convenience.

Tuition fees and value — review tuition alongside uniforms, activities and transportation.

Special needs support — schools offering tailored programs and resources significantly impact a child's success.

Top International Schools

Singapore American School (SAS) — American curriculum from preschool through high school, SGD 25,000–40,000 annually. Woodlands campus with AP programs, science labs, sports and performing arts.

Stamford American International School — combines American and IB curricula, SGD 45,000–52,000. Novena campus with personalized learning, special needs and EAL support.

XCL American Academy — American curriculum at SGD 25,000–32,000 from K–Grade 8 in Yishun.

International Community School — American curriculum with Christian values, SGD 28,000–40,000 in Jurong.

Canadian International School — IB and Ontario curriculum, SGD 19,000–54,000 across two campuses.

Overseas Family School — IB programs, SGD 17,000–44,000, serving over 70 nationalities.

United World College of South East Asia (UWCSEA) — IB curriculum, SGD 51,000–56,000 across Dover and East Coast campuses, emphasis on global citizenship.

Nexus International School — IB and British curriculum, SGD 34,000–45,000 in Aljunied, eco-friendly campus.

Chatsworth International School — boutique IB and British curriculum, SGD 29,000–39,000 in Bukit Timah.

ISS International School — IB curriculum, SGD 32,000–55,000 in a central location.

One World International School — affordable IB, SGD 21,000–22,000, focus on sustainability.

Sir Manasseh Meyer International School — British and International Primary Curriculum, SGD 18,000–29,000 in Redhill.

EtonHouse International School — IB PYP and British curriculum, SGD 16,000–20,000 across multiple campuses.

Dulwich College (Singapore) — British curriculum, SGD 19,000–54,000 in Bukit Batok.

Tanglin Trust School — British curriculum, SGD 33,000–53,000 in One-North.

Dover Court International School — personalized British curriculum with IB Diploma options, SGD 27,000–43,000.

Brighton College (Singapore) — British-based, SGD 17,000–57,000 in East Coast.

Australian International School — Australian curriculum, SGD 36,000–50,000 in Lorong Chuan with Olympic-sized pools.

Conclusion

Each institution offers unique strengths and specialized programs catering to different needs. The American Club provides networking opportunities for expat families seeking insights and community support during the school selection process.

title: Swimming Class for Kids and Lessons in Singapore
excerpt: Singapore offers diverse swimming schools for children of all ages — from infant classes to competitive training. A guide to top programs to help parents choose the right fit.
body:
Why Enrol Your Child?

Singapore's environment, filled with recreational water spaces, makes swimming an essential life skill. Early lessons build crucial water safety habits and lifelong confidence in pools, beaches and waterparks.

Swimming offers a full-body workout — strengthening muscles, enhancing coordination and promoting cardiovascular health, while being easy on growing joints. It also nurtures discipline, self-esteem and emotional growth.

For Babies and Toddlers

The American Club's Infants & Toddlers Swimming Program — designed by infant aquatic specialists. From four months to three years, with curriculum based on age-specific motor skills. (10 Claymore Hill | 6739-4450 | aquatics@amclub.org.sg)

SWISH! Swimming (Dempsey Hill) — heated, salt-chlorinated pools from four months old. Year-round classes including 'Auntie and Me' sessions and Merfit. (72 Loewen Road, #01-08 | 9832 2522)

Inspire Mum & Baby (Tanjong Katong) — aqua therapy from two months old. Aqua Tub program replicates the womb. Lessons up to age seven with SwimSafer and STA Certification progression. (188-2 Tanjong Katong Road | 9234 1866)

Popular Schools for Kids

Swim Masters — gentle, supportive environment for anxious learners. Led by Coach Alvin with flexible monthly make-ups. Caters from one year old with private lessons available. (Senja Cashew Swimming Complex | 9060 1817 / 6709 0018)

aquaDucks — established 1989, multiple locations. Blends European, American and Australian methods. From four months old, with a competitive swim team for ages 8–14. (Queenstown, Mountbatten, Newton, Normanton, Pasir Panjang | 6463 5554)

Professional Programs

SwimAmerica at The American Club — developed by the American Swimming Coaches Association, drawing on techniques used by USA Olympic champions. Structured, results-driven, beginner-friendly. (10 Claymore Hill | 6739 4450)

Pacific Swim Team — National Age Group Championships success across age groups. From age four, with extra lifeguard supervision during classes. (1 Tampines Walk, Our Tampines Hub | 9856 8280)

Top Indoor Schools

Marsden Swim School — 20+ years of experience, from four months old. Hot showers, changing rooms, infant shower seats. Singapore Swimming Association affiliated. (Jalan Jurong Kechil / GESS / Delta Swimming Complex | 6015 0637)

Happy Fish Swim School — established 2007, heated indoor pools, all ages from four months. Special needs classes, prenatal sessions, and lifesaving courses. (Bedok, Jurong East, Bukit Timah | 6589 8650)

How to Choose

Location — close to home or easily accessible.

Convenience — schedule flexibility, make-up lessons, heated pools and indoor options.

Instructor qualifications — SwimSafer or recognised association certification; specialised programs where needed.

Safety — lifeguard-certified instructors, well-maintained facilities, proper supervision ratios and water-safety education.

Ready to get your child started? Explore the top-notch swimming programs at The American Club.

title: The American Club Membership: Benefits and What You Can Expect
excerpt: The American Club offers American and Canadian expats in Singapore a unique private community with world-class facilities, diverse dining options and extensive sports offerings.
body:
Relocating to a new country can be overwhelming. The American Club Membership offers a unique community of American and Canadian expats in Singapore.

Close-Knit American and Canadian Expat Community

Membership means joining a close-knit community of American and Canadian expats. For business owners, this is a valuable opportunity to build relationships with professionals who share similar cultural and business experiences.

Member Engagement events and an exclusive Business Center provide a nurturing environment to connect, collaborate, and exchange ideas — fostering personal connections and professional growth.

World-Class Facilities

Thinkspace and the Business Center are perfect for professionals seeking a quiet space to work or hold meetings.

Junior Members enjoy The Quad — a four-story recreational area for children of all ages, with a vertical climbing wall, treehouse, computer gaming zone and more.

In 2019 the Club completed a major refurbishment of its 120,000-square-foot clubhouse — modernising the space for greater comfort and convenience.

Extensive Sports Offerings

From water sports and tennis to Pickleball, there's something for everyone. The fully equipped gym features state-of-the-art equipment, with professional instructors and coaches on hand.

SwimAmerica, the premier learn-to-swim program developed by the American Swimming Coaches Association, offers expert coaching for all ages and skill levels. Members also enjoy a private bowling alley and tennis courts.

World-Class Dining

Tradewinds offers global dishes for family dinners or business lunches. The 2nd Floor combines Eastern and Western flavors for a sophisticated dining experience. Union Bar is the classic American sports bar to unwind in after work with the latest game on.

Spa

Sên Spa features full-body treatments — massages, facials, and the 'Sên Loyalty Package' that rewards regular visitors with special perks.

For Children and Families

The Quad caters to children of various ages with safe, exciting spaces to play and make friends. Kids camps focus on fun and skill development, with engaging activities and a structured, nurturing environment.

Membership Fees

Ordinary Membership: $32,530.00 — for American and Canadian expats aged 21+.
Service Membership: $19,057.50.
Term Membership: $10,286.00.
Transferable Membership: $32,700.00.

Junior Membership is available for individuals aged 12–25.

FAQ

Can Singaporeans join? Singaporeans can join via Associate Membership — available to individuals over 21 residing in Singapore who don't qualify for Ordinary Membership.

Is the Club open to the public? The Club is members-only; non-members are welcome only when accompanied by a current Member.

Is it worth it? The Club offers an unparalleled experience for American expats — exceptional service, networking, and premium amenities create a space that feels both professional and personal.

title: Best Family-Friendly Social Clubs & Country Clubs in Singapore
excerpt: Singapore's social clubs offer expat families welcoming communities with premier amenities, dining experiences and activities. A look at The American Club, Seletar Country Club and other top venues.
body:
Singapore is a premier destination for expat families seeking to establish themselves in a dynamic global city. Social clubs throughout the island nation provide essential support systems — helping newcomers connect with fellow expats while accessing world-class recreational and dining facilities.

The American Club

A flagship establishment for US expats and their families. Diverse dining venues, state-of-the-art fitness centers, swimming pools, and the exclusive SwimAmerica learn-to-swim program. Membership from S$10,286 (term). The Club emphasises family integration through youth activities including arts, crafts, sports leagues and holiday camps. (10 Claymore Hill)

Seletar Country Club

Well-maintained golf courses and family events in the north of the island.

Orchid Country Club

Extensive golf facilities and the family-friendly Orchid Bowl.

The British Club

Blends British traditions with Singapore's multicultural environment — tennis, squash and swimming for families.

Specialised Sports Clubs

Singapore Swimming Club for aquatic activities and family dining. Singapore Polo Club for equestrian experiences and wellness facilities. Sailing-focused venues like Changi Sailing Club with chalets and recreational boating.

Together, these establishments provide comprehensive social ecosystems — combining recreation, networking, cultural events and dining that ease the transition for expat families relocating to Singapore.

title: Still Waters Run Deep
excerpt: Greg Fasala, Olympic medalist and Head Aquatics Coach, leads the Club's revitalised Aquatics Program with determination and vision after the facility's redevelopment.
body:
If you're an avid swimmer at the Club, it's likely that Greg Fasala, Team Manager and Head Coach of Aquatics, is no stranger to you. A ubiquitous fixture in and by the pool with an exuberant personality and reassuring presence, Greg brings to his role as much warmth as he does an outstanding portfolio with some of the most revered accolades in the sporting world.

Greg's sunny disposition belies a steely resilience and unflinchingly resolute determination to see his plans through from aspiration to achievement — an approach to life which earned him an Olympic silver medal for the 4×100-metre freestyle relay at the 1984 Summer Olympics, and three Gold medals across two Commonwealth Games.

Greg recounts training for the big leagues as a student athlete, spending hours on end each day perfecting his form in the pool and working out everywhere else. With singular devotion to honing his talent, it would not be unusual to find Greg doing laps way before sunrise — school day or otherwise.

Guided by the principles of hard work and persistence, Greg shared two quotes close to his heart — "The only place success comes before work is in the dictionary", and "winning is not everything, but wanting to win is". These sayings provide an interesting exposition of the person Greg is — pragmatic and results-oriented, but also one who understands that the process is often more transformative than its outcome.

All of Greg's traits — visionary, determined, steadfast, and prudent — have served him well throughout his competitive and coaching career, taking him from wins at the Olympics and Commonwealth Games, studies in the Australian Institute of Sports and numerous high-profile coaching jobs to his current role at the Club. Aside from helming the development of the Club's Aquatics training programs, Greg oversees a team of 13 Team Members across coaching, watersports and lifeguarding.

In 2019, following the Club's redevelopment and the unveiling of its new swimming complex, Greg took on a leadership role in rebuilding the Aquatics Program. The task was daunting — all swimming and water-based activities had to be put on hold during the renovation period. But Greg saw an opportunity to raise the standing of the program by focusing on human capital and introducing innovative, world-class elements to the curriculum.

A strategic component of this rebranding was to establish a partnership with SwimAmerica, the premier learn-to-swim program operated by the American Swimming Coaches Association. Greg, Assistant Coach Hariz Zailani, and Aquatics Coordinator Zack Leong earned the honor of becoming the only three SwimAmerica Program Directors in Southeast Asia — making the Club the sole facility in the region qualified to run the SwimAmerica Program.

This, alongside the newly constructed state-of-the-art physical amenities, solidified the Club's reputation as one of the leading venues for all things Aquatics — whether for recreational or competitive pursuits.

Greg attributes a large part of his ability to see this hefty project through to the support of his team. "I am fortunate to have individuals like Chief Lifeguard Abdul, one of the Club's longest-serving staff members, who has been a source of wisdom for other Team Members, including me".

While extremely proud of the program's success, Greg is even more moved by the heart and soul of it — his team. Previously unaccustomed to relying on others, he now thrives on the spirited camaraderie. After all, he has known from the very start that the journey to the finish line is often more transformative than the crossing of it. And that's a real win in life.

title: Affordable International Schools in Singapore
excerpt: Singapore offers world-class international education for expat families, though tuition costs vary significantly. A guide to estimated expenses and reasonably priced schools.
body:
Singapore stands as a dream destination for expat families seeking quality education. The city-state boasts world-class infrastructure, multicultural environments, and holistic learning approaches — though educational costs extend beyond tuition alone.

Estimated Educational Costs

Application fees range from 30 to 5,000 SGD depending on education level. Annual tuition varies by stage — preschool (5,000–38,000 SGD), primary school (13,000–42,000 SGD), and secondary school (15,000–49,000 SGD).

Additional expenses include uniforms (300–400 SGD per set), transportation via school bus (1,000–8,000 SGD annually) or public transport (900–1,500 SGD yearly), extracurricular activities (25–100 SGD hourly), and school lunches (1,000–1,500 SGD annually).

Cost-Reduction Strategies

Families can minimise expenses by selecting schools near their residence, exploring scholarships and financial aid, and creating comprehensive budgets. Many schools offer sibling discounts and early-bird payment incentives.

Top Affordable Schools

Australian International School — early-years fees from 9,000–18,000 SGD per semester, with 300+ co-curricular activities.

One World International School — tuition from 21,034 SGD for early childhood to 22,857 SGD for Grade 12, with a Chinese–English bilingual programme.

Invictus International School — 20,800–22,011 SGD annually with Cambridge curricula and bilingual options.

Knightsbridge House International School — competitive pricing from 13,080 SGD yearly with strong Cambridge results and a diverse student population across two campuses.

title: Celebrate 4th of July at The American Club
excerpt: The American Club hosts the 249th anniversary celebration of American independence on July 4, 2025 — featuring BBQ, family activities, and live entertainment with the American Association of Singapore.
body:
Staying in Singapore this summer? You won't miss out on the most patriotic day of the year. There's no better place to celebrate America's independence than right here at The American Club — your Home Away From Home. Gather your family and friends for a fun-filled Fourth, surrounded by the warmth of our vibrant, close-knit American community.

This year marks the 249th anniversary of America's independence. Organised by the American Association of Singapore (AAS), the Club is proud to be the host venue for an unforgettable birthday bash, complete with festive red, white and blue flair.

Get ready to indulge in iconic American favourites — smoky BBQ ribs and spicy wings, beef brisket tacos, southern fried chicken, and the ultimate crowd-pleaser: classic apple pie.

Don't forget to save room for one of the day's most beloved traditions — the Watermelon Eating Contest. This juicy, messy, laugh-out-loud showdown is perfect for participants and spectators of all ages.

Little ones can look forward to a host of family-friendly activities, including bouncy castles, patriotic crafts, and splash-tastic pool games. The celebration continues with toe-tapping Dixieland tunes from the New Stream Brass Band, a stirring Color Guard ceremony by the US Navy, and a dual national anthem performance by Vy Vallejo, a graduating student from the Singapore American School.

Don your most spirited red, white and blue and strike a pose at our Harley-Davidson-themed photo booth — you might just take home the title of Best Dressed Individual or Group.

Event Details

Date: Friday, July 4, 2025
Time: 4:00 PM – 9:00 PM
Venue: The American Club, 10 Claymore Hill, Singapore 229573

title: Family-Friendly Places to Visit in Singapore During Spring Break
excerpt: Spring Break (late March to early April) is ideal for exploring Singapore's attractions. Top destinations for kids — from wildlife parks to interactive museums — plus the Club's Youth Spring Camp.
body:
Spring Break (end of March to the first week of April) is the ideal time for families to explore Singapore's many exciting and educational attractions.

Bird Paradise

A visit to Bird Paradise is unforgettable for nature-loving families. Home to over 3,500 birds from around the world, the park offers lush, expansive habitats that allow kids to observe exotic species up close. From vibrant flamingos to playful penguins, the wide array of birds and interactive exhibits make it an ideal destination for children to learn about wildlife conservation while having fun. Don't miss the live bird shows and feeding sessions.
Address: 20 Mandai Lake Road

KidZania

At KidZania, children experience the world of grown-ups through role-playing — becoming doctors, pilots, bakers and firefighters. They'll earn KidZos (the in-park currency) to spend on souvenirs or save up for more activities. A fantastic way for kids to develop responsibility, decision-making and teamwork while having a blast in an immersive environment.
Address: 31 Beach View Road, #01-01/02 Sentosa Island, Singapore 098008

Hay Dairies Goat Farm

A hands-on, rural experience. Just outside the city, kids can join guided tours to learn about goat care, milk production and sustainable farming, and have the chance to feed and interact with the goats. Sample fresh goat milk on-site.
Address: 250 Neo Tiew Cresent, Singapore 719866

Children's Museum Singapore

Hands-on exhibits cover topics from Singapore's history and culture to art and technology. Themed galleries stimulate creativity and curiosity through interactive displays, with workshops designed to capture younger children's imaginations.
Address: 23-B Coleman Street, Singapore 179807

The American Club Youth Spring Camp

For parents who are busy during Spring Break, the Club's Youth Spring Camp — available for two different age groups starting from four years old — offers a fun and safe environment for children to engage in exciting activities. From arts and crafts to sports and games, the camp keeps kids entertained while building new skills and friendships, with expert supervision and age-appropriate activities throughout.

title: From Sausage Rolls to Sweet Treats: Holiday Traditions Unwrapped
excerpt: Members share cherished Christmas traditions — from multicultural feasts blending German, American and British customs to festive recipes and cosy décor tips that make the season memorable.
body:
As the festive season approaches, the American Club highlights how Members celebrate through diverse traditions.

Shaun & Alexandra

Shaun and his wife Alexandra blend German, American, and British customs. Christmas Eve features homemade sausage rolls alongside German Kartoffelsalat and Würstchen. Their Christmas morning includes champagne, orange juice, gift-opening in pyjamas, and a brunch of American pancakes and traditional German Stollen fruit bread. Their home décor combines delicate German glass ornaments with handmade decorations and a nativity scene.

Shaun's Sausage Rolls

Mix sausage meat with chopped onion, grated apple, sage, thyme, salt and pepper. Wrap in puff pastry strips, cut into 5cm pieces, brush with beaten egg, and bake at 200°C for 20–25 minutes until golden brown.

Alison's American Christmas

Alison shares cherished traditions developed during three years in the United States. Lamb rack is her signature festive dish — seared in olive oil, coated with a breadcrumb–garlic–rosemary mixture, and baked at 450°F for 12–18 minutes until the internal temperature reaches 130°F. She also bakes decorated gingerbread cookies as a family activity.

For décor, Alison recommends abundant lighting for warmth, displaying a cosy fireplace scene on television, and playing Christmas music throughout. She personalises stockings with family names and adorns her Christmas tree with handmade ornaments from her daughters — creating an inviting atmosphere completed by sharing hot cocoa together.

title: Insights into Singapore's Work Culture to Prepare for a Seamless Transition
excerpt: Singapore blends Eastern and Western cultural influences to create a unique professional environment. Understanding local workplace norms is essential for expats navigating career transitions.
body:
Singapore is the melting pot of Eastern and Western culture, which shapes the professional environment governed by unwritten rules and regulations. Understanding these cultural nuances is essential for individuals navigating transitions within the Singaporean workforce.

Dress Code and Professionalism

How you dress profoundly influences your professional image and how superiors perceive you. Dress codes vary by industry and company culture, so acquaint yourself with your workplace's requirements when starting a new job.

Teamwork Over Individualism

In Singaporean organisations, prioritising teamwork over individualism is fundamental — affecting decision-making and how teams operate. This contrasts sharply with Western cultures, where companies often emphasise individual achievements.

Frameworks of Rules and Regulations

Clear rules serve as the foundation for efficient operations. The management board plays a crucial role in guiding company performance and prioritising alignment with directives from higher management. Employees are encouraged to explore creativity within a supportive structure.

Work Hours

The standard workweek typically spans 40 to 45 hours, with flexibility for workload fluctuations. Employees have half an hour to an hour for lunch. The Employment Act states that covered employees cannot be required to work more than 12 hours a day, with monthly overtime limited to 72 hours.

Diverse Workforce

Singapore's multicultural society fosters an exceptionally diverse workforce — with over 1.1 million international residents making up one-third of the total workforce and over 7,000 multinational companies operating in the nation.

Why Expats Choose Singapore

Singapore's inclusive nature makes it conducive for foreigners to work and integrate into society; the country's development strategy prioritises attracting foreign talent, resulting in lucrative compensation packages; a personal tax rate of 15% for foreign employees plus exemptions for income earned abroad facilitates rapid financial stability; and one of the world's strongest economies provides ample career prospects.

Singapore vs. US Work Cultures

In Singapore, avoid pointing at things or using your feet to gesture — it can be considered rude. Expressing disagreement with superiors may be seen as ambition in the US, but it's generally frowned upon in Singapore. Individuality is encouraged in the US; group success is prioritised in Singapore. Negotiations in Singapore typically involve longer processes — companies follow internal hierarchies before reaching a final decision maker.

Cultural Etiquette

Avoid discussing sensitive topics like politics, religion, crime or punishment. Refrain from physical contact with individuals of the opposite sex. Avoid pointing directly at someone with your finger. Respect personal boundaries by not touching someone's head or face. Avoid standing with your hands on your hips — it can convey aggression.

Singaporeans place strong emphasis on punctuality, responsibility, and adherence to rules. A standard working day is eight hours, lunch excluded. Continuous work for up to eight hours requires a break of at least 45 minutes.

While Singapore has a competitive job market, it remains relatively accessible for foreigners — with government support and resources to help foreign workers acclimate to the local work environment.

title: Cost of Living in Singapore: Managing Expenses in One of the World's Most Expensive Cities
excerpt: Singapore ranks among the top destinations for expats, but understanding the cost of living is essential. A guide to accommodation, transport, education and other key expenses for relocating to the city-state.
body:
Singapore is renowned for its exceptional quality of life and consistently attracts expats and locals. However, this standard comes at a premium.

Transportation

Manageable with an average one-way ticket fare of approximately S$2 for buses and MRT. Daily commuting totals around S$4, or roughly S$80 monthly. Taxis are reliable, with fares starting at S$3–3.40 for the first mile.

Accommodation

Varies by location and property condition. Rental costs depend on proximity to the city centre and available amenities.

Education

Free for Singaporean citizens but costs S$205 monthly for Permanent Residents. International schools charge S$12,000 to S$30,000 annually, while childcare ranges from S$400 to S$1,500 per month.

Entertainment

Gym memberships cost around S$100 monthly and movie tickets range from S$9–13.

Internet and Mobile

Plans range from S$35 to S$100 per month, with residential internet averaging S$50. Utility bills typically run S$200–600 monthly depending on air conditioning usage.

Groceries

Local products are relatively affordable. Milk runs about S$3.95 per litre, rice S$3.65 per kilogram.

Healthcare

World-class but expensive for expats without coverage. Many opt for employer-provided or private insurance.

Tax Benefits

Progressive tax rates range from 0% to 20% for residents staying 183+ days, and a flat 15% rate for shorter stays.

Cost Reduction Strategies

Use public transportation, find affordable housing in less expensive areas, shop at local markets, and utilise free community events and parks.

title: Types of Housing in Singapore and its Costs
excerpt: Singapore offers diverse housing options for expats — from affordable HDB flats to luxurious private condominiums and landed properties. A guide to different residential types and their costs.
body:
As a melting pot of cultures, Singapore offers a diverse range of housing options to accommodate the varied needs of expats.

Public Housing (HDB)

HDB flats, built by the Housing and Development Board, offer affordable and convenient housing for Singapore residents. They function as self-contained townships incorporating schools, parks, supermarkets, shopping malls and MRT stations. While primarily intended for Singaporean residents, expats can rent HDB flats through real estate agents. Options include 2 to 5-room flats (1–4 bedrooms), studio apartments with open-concept layouts, and 3Gen flats designed for multi-generation families.

Private Housing

Condominiums are apartment buildings designed for private ownership with shared amenities such as swimming pools and gyms. They come in two types — low-rise walk-up condominiums offering intimate living with lower maintenance fees, and high-rise buildings featuring luxurious amenities but higher costs.

Landed Properties

Diverse housing options including terrace houses (rows with shared walls), shophouses (commercial spaces on lower floors with residential above), semi-detached houses (sharing a common wall), and townhouses (landed homes with communal facilities).

Co-Sharing Living Spaces

Co-living embodies communal living — individuals occupy private bedrooms and bathrooms while sharing common areas like kitchens and living rooms. With a minimum three-month stay requirement, these fully furnished spaces appeal to individuals, couples, and students seeking affordable community living.

Average Housing Costs

HDB flats average S$633,166 (87.6 square metres). Condominium units average S$2.57 million (89 square metres). Landed properties average S$9.39 million (405 square metres).

Finding the right home in Singapore is crucial for expats. With strict housing regulations and a competitive rental market, many rely on professional real estate agents or relocation services to ensure a smooth transition.
